title: Gulf Flight Suspensions Stretch to October; Istanbul Steps Up
excerpt: British Airways, Singapore Airlines and others have pushed Dubai/Gulf flight suspensions to Sept-Oct 2026, while Turkish Airlines expands via Istanbul.
content:
If your long-haul routing depends on Dubai, Doha or another Gulf hub later this summer, the picture has shifted again this week, and not in the direction anyone hoped. The US-Iran conflict that first disrupted Gulf air travel in the spring reignited on July 12 and, as of July 18, has run seven consecutive nights of strikes, with Iran reporting more than 50 deaths and 500 injuries, two US troops killed and one missing after an attack in Jordan, and damage to an oil facility and power plant in Kuwait. Oil prices have climbed past $86 a barrel on reduced Strait of Hormuz shipping activity. None of this has closed the major Gulf airports, but it has pushed a growing list of long-haul carriers to abandon their earlier restart dates in favor of much longer suspensions.

As of July 15, at least thirteen international airlines had extended their Dubai suspensions well past their original summer restart plans. British Airways now has Dubai off its schedule until October 25, and Singapore Airlines, Air Canada, AirBaltic and ITA Airways have all pushed their own Dubai suspensions to October 24 - a sharp jump from the early-August restart Singapore Airlines had been promising just two weeks earlier. Lufthansa Group (covering Lufthansa, SWISS, Austrian and Brussels Airlines) is holding its Dubai suspension to September 13 but has other Gulf destinations, including Abu Dhabi, Riyadh, Dammam and Muscat, off the board until October 24. Wizz Air has Dubai and Abu Dhabi suspended into mid-September, and Aegean has Dubai suspended through August 31. Several Asian carriers, including Japan Airlines' Tokyo-Doha route, have separately extended their own Gulf suspensions into the same August-September window. The pattern across nearly every one of these airlines is the same: each new decision point has produced a longer pause than the one before it, so treat any restart date on this list as provisional rather than final.

Set against that retreat, one long-haul connector has gone the opposite way. Turkish Airlines has spent July methodically restoring the exact routes other carriers are abandoning, using Istanbul as the workaround. It resumed Abu Dhabi service on July 1, added Dammam on July 10, brought back Kuwait City on July 11, and restarted Bahrain on July 16, on top of a Dubai frequency that already doubled from 7 to 14 weekly flights back in June. Amman and Beirut frequencies were also increased earlier in the summer. SunExpress, the Turkish Airlines-Lufthansa joint venture, resumed its Antalya-Dubai route on July 15 as well. Istanbul's appeal in a conflict like this one is straightforward: it sits outside the airspace corridors currently flagged as highest-risk by European regulators, so it can keep serving Gulf capitals on relatively direct routings while carriers based in Western Europe and Asia reroute around restricted airspace or simply cancel.

That said, don't treat Istanbul as untouchably safe just because it's currently the exception. Turkish Airlines itself has said further cancellations are possible if regional airspace conditions deteriorate, and this same conflict has already shown it can escalate with only days of warning - the current seven-night run started from what many airlines had treated as a settled situation as recently as late June.

So what should you actually do? If you're holding a ticket on British Airways, Singapore Airlines, Lufthansa Group, Wizz Air, Aegean or a similar carrier for Dubai or another Gulf destination in August or early September, check your booking now rather than waiting for the airline to email you - several of these suspensions were extended with only about two weeks' notice, and a further push is plausible. If you need a working connection to Abu Dhabi, Dammam, Kuwait City or Bahrain in the near term and your usual carrier has paused service, Turkish Airlines via Istanbul is currently the more reliable option among major international connectors, though it's worth booking a fare class or ticket type that allows changes given how fluid this remains. Anyone connecting through Dubai or Doha on a Gulf-based carrier (Emirates, Etihad, Qatar Airways, flydubai) should still expect day-to-day delays rather than outright route suspensions, so reconfirm your specific flight 24-48 hours out. And whichever hub you route through, check that your travel insurance actually covers conflict-related cancellations before you rely on it, since standard policies often carve out exactly this kind of disruption.
